one_line_takeaway: A position paper (not an implemented system) that proposes and qualitatively motivates using a small trusted eFPGA fabric embedded in an ASIC LLM accelerator to enable IP redaction, PUF-bound configuration, reconfigurable side-channel resistance, a lightweight TEE, and runtime-monitored post-deployment patching against side-channel, fault-injection, and supply-chain (Trojan/IP-piracy) attacks.
key_finding: The paper's central claim (a qualitative judgment, not a measured result) is that a hybrid ASIC+eFPGA architecture occupies a favorable middle ground: it retains most of the ASIC's compute efficiency (rated 'medium/••' vs. 'high/•••' for ASIC-only and 'low/•' for FPGA-only in Table III) while gaining substantially more security flexibility, post-deployment update capability, side-channel-resistance adaptability, and supply-chain protection than an ASIC-only design (each rated 'medium/••' or higher for the hybrid vs. 'low/•' for ASIC-only). Among the five individual mitigation strategies (Table II), S1 (IP redaction) and S2 (encrypted/device-bound configuration) are both rated as already 'Mature' with low-to-medium hardware/performance cost, while S3 (side-channel resistance via reconfiguration) is rated 'Emerging' and S4 (eFPGA-based TEE) and S5 (runtime monitors/patching) are rated 'Research'-stage with S4 specifically flagged as incurring the highest performance and resource cost of all five strategies.
core_contribution: This is a short (5-page) special-session position/vision paper (accepted at IEEE VTS 2026) proposing a hybrid ASIC+eFPGA architecture for edge LLM inference accelerators, where a small embedded FPGA (eFPGA) fabric is added alongside the ASIC datapath to serve as a reconfigurable security module. The paper's own contribution is threefold: (1) a threat model and attack-vector taxonomy (Table I) mapping known hardware attacks against ASIC-based DNN/LLM accelerators to the LLM accelerator components they affect and to eFPGA-based mitigations; (2) an articulation of five eFPGA-enabled mitigation strategies (S1-S5: selective IP redaction, encrypted/device-bound eFPGA configuration via PUF, side-channel resistance via reconfigurable fabric, heterogeneous computation on an eFPGA-based TEE, and runtime monitoring with post-deployment patching); and (3) a qualitative design-space comparison (Tables II and III) rating these strategies and rating ASIC-only vs. FPGA-only vs. hybrid ASIC+eFPGA accelerators on security, adaptability, hardware/performance cost, and maturity using a coarse low/medium/high (dot) scale. The paper explicitly frames itself as an architectural/conceptual proposal ('we advocate for the integration of...', 'we outline five key mitigation strategies') rather than an implemented and measured system.
threat_model: Adversary can exploit hardware/microarchitectural vulnerabilities in ASIC-based DNN accelerators across design, fabrication, and runtime phases: (1) remote attacker in multi-tenant environments (e.g., co-located cloud FPGA/accelerator tenants performing power-plundering or remote side-channel attacks); (2) attacker with physical access performing side-channel (power, EM, timing) and fault-injection attacks (voltage manipulation, clock glitches, memory perturbation, bit flips in quantization scaling factors); (3) supply-chain adversary acting through untrusted third-party IP or the fabrication process, inserting hardware Trojans or performing reverse engineering/IP piracy/counterfeiting. The paper assumes the base ASIC 'may not be fully trusted' while the integrated eFPGA fabric is trusted (via secure boot) and used to monitor, detect, and mitigate threats. Assets identified: model weights and quantization metadata, KV-cache (user prompts/context), and control-plane artifacts (microcode, scheduling heuristics), with security goals spanning confidentiality, integrity, and availability (e.g., preventing output corruption, targeted manipulation/DoS, and stealthy long-term degradation from Trojan triggers).
limitations: The authors themselves note that ensuring robust protection via S5 'requires carefully calibrated detection thresholds to avoid false positives, as well as verifiable patch pipelines and formal constraints to prevent misuse of the reconfigurable fabric' -- i.e., an unsolved design problem. More broadly (not stated by the authors as a limitations section, but evident from the paper's own framing): the entire evaluation (Tables II and III) consists of coarse three-level qualitative dot ratings assigned by the authors rather than any simulated or measured area/power/latency overhead, attack success rate, or side-channel leakage metric; no accelerator, eFPGA fabric, or attack was actually built, synthesized, or tested. The threat model assumes the eFPGA fabric itself is trusted via secure boot, which is asserted rather than justified or evaluated. S4 (eFPGA TEE) is explicitly flagged by the authors' own table as 'Research' maturity with the highest hardware and performance cost among all five strategies, i.e., the strongest-guarantee mechanism is the least mature and most expensive.

quantitative_results: No quantitative experimental results are reported. Tables II and III use only a coarse 3-level qualitative dot scale (low/medium/high) assigned by the authors to rate security, adaptability, hardware cost, performance cost, and maturity of five proposed mitigation strategies (Table II) and of ASIC-only vs. FPGA-only vs. hybrid ASIC+eFPGA architectures across six dimensions (Table III).
baselines_compared: ASIC-only accelerator (rated in Table III: high compute efficiency, low security flexibility, low post-deployment update capability, low SCR adaptability, medium supply-chain protection, low silicon area cost)
FPGA-only accelerator (rated in Table III: low compute efficiency, high security flexibility, high post-deployment updates, high SCR adaptability, low supply-chain protection, high silicon area cost)

Hybrid ASIC+eFPGA (the paper's proposed architecture, rated in Table III: medium compute efficiency, medium security flexibility, medium post-deployment updates, medium SCR adaptability, high supply-chain protection, medium silicon area cost)
